Aging widebody aircraft like the 767 remain economically viable for high-net-worth individuals when refurbished, extending the commercial lifecycle of older frames beyond typical retirement schedules.

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

Here’s How Much Wider The Airbus A350 Cabin Is Compared To The Boeing 787 Dreamliner

The Airbus A350 cabin is 5 inches wider than the Boeing 787—here’s how that small difference transforms seat comfort and long-haul travel.

The A350's 5-inch cabin width advantage enables 10-abreast seating versus 787's 9-abreast, fundamentally reshaping airline revenue models and passenger comfort trade-offs on widebody routes.

Artemis II Astronauts Launch to Moon

NASA’s Space Launch System rocket and Orion spacecraft lift off in this April 1, 2026, image. NASA’s Artemis II mission will take NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy on an approximately 10-day journey around the Moon and...

Artemis II's April 2026 launch represents NASA's critical validation of human lunar transportation systems before sustained Moon operations begin.

Washington Repeals Aircraft Tax Before It Takes Effect

Measure enacted in 2025 is replaced with fuel tax and fee increases.

Washington's shift from aircraft purchase tax to fuel-based taxation could reduce upfront aircraft acquisition costs while increasing operating expenses for regular users.

Military Apr 2, 2026

Global Combat Air Programme Awards First Contract with Edgewing

The first joint international contract has been awarded for the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), focusing on key design and engineering activities. The GCAP International Government Organisation (GIGO), which represents the governments of the UK, Italy and Japan, awarded a £686 million (about...

The £686 million contract signals that the UK-Italy-Japan fighter jet partnership is moving from planning to active development, establishing Edgewing as a critical design authority for a major military aviation program.

Geopolitical instability is forcing airlines to reassess avionics redundancy and routing strategies, creating unanticipated operational costs that traditional risk models underestimated.

Industry Apr 2, 2026

Avionics manufacturers are shifting from traditional hardware-centric architectures to software-defined systems, reducing maintenance costs and enabling faster capability updates across fleet operations.

Industry Apr 2, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization through advanced technologies offers airlines a near-term pathway to reduce environmental impact while meeting projected traffic growth without major infrastructure expansion.

See MDPI →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

Climate Change Is Rewriting the Rules of Turbulence—and Cockpits Are Playing Catch-Up

Global Avionics Round-Up from Aircraft Value News (AVN) For decades, turbulence was treated as a known nuisance—unpredictable, occasionally dangerous, but broadly manageable within the design assumptions of modern aircraft. Engineers […]...

Climate-driven atmospheric changes are exceeding the turbulence parameters that current aircraft systems were designed for, requiring fundamental updates to avionics and structural standards.

Military Apr 2, 2026

Army’s SkyFoundry Plan To Build Own Cheap Drones Has ‘Evolved,’ Official Notes Commercial Involvement

The Army’s SkyFoundry initiative to manufacture thousands of its own cheap drones on a monthly basis has recently “evolved,” with an official telling sister publication Defense Daily the program is “100 […]...

The Army's shift toward commercial partnerships in SkyFoundry signals potential cost reductions and manufacturing scalability that could reshape military drone procurement models.

Autonomous flight integration through Prism-Hivemind software marks critical progress toward unmanned military aircraft certification under the CCA program.

Military Apr 2, 2026

Army Accepts First Optionally-Piloted Black Hawk, Will Inform Scaling Autonomy Across The Fleet

The Army announced Friday it has officially accepted the first Black Hawk helicopter outfitted to fly autonomously, with the plans for the aircraft to now undergo a “rigorous testing phase.” […]...

The Army's acceptance of its first autonomously-capable Black Hawk marks a critical validation milestone that will establish operational protocols and safety standards for scaling autonomous systems across thousands of existing rotorcraft.

5 Reasons Why The Boeing 777X Needs Folding Wingtips

The Boeing 777X's folding wingtips are a critical solution to a fundamental design conflict between airline efficiency and airport infrastructure.

Folding wingtips resolve the 777X's core tension: airlines want larger, fuel-efficient frames while airports lack gate infrastructure for wider aircraft.

FAA Absent from Cleveland Burke Lakefront Closure Hearing

Why Seat Width & Pitch Vary So Much Between US Airlines

No two airlines in the US are really quite the same, especially in terms of seats. What makes each airline so drastically different?

Airlines optimize cabin density differently based on route profitability and competitive positioning, with no FAA minimum standards governing seat dimensions.
